item.title: Site Criticism Team - Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Shaunak Nandigam, the thread of another article is not the place for this kind of comment, much less as a reply to a comment from 2018. Do not do this in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wow, my first, second and third and fourth articles were deleted for low rating

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love everything about the great attractor (whatever it may actually be), though I wish there were a little bit more to it… I also wonder if the description being so similar to the Wikipedia entry was on purpose or accidental.

However, the image of an intergalactic Kirby monster that comes to mind every time I read the event log is enough for me to upvote.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article comes from here, which allows for reuse of images for any purpose, making it cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I… Really don't find this compelling. Basing your article on an existing thing is totally fine. In this case, however, the article doesn't seem transformative enough to stand on its own at all. I didn't see enough details that would suggest that you've expanded on the topic, so the end result feels more like a description of the real attractor (and some theories regarding it) in SCP format. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is good! Just wanted to mention you are missing a period at the end of the procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…the Foundation knows something big is there but it can't properly see it because our galaxy's in the way.

Which is not so different from what is known in real life. Like I said, the article is reiterating things. It doesn't offer anything exclusive that only the Foundation knows except for it destroying a galaxy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, reading that and seeing the image again, that makes sense. It's a big, scary space thing, and big scary space things are terrifying! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The acronym is a case where truth is allowed to be stranger than fiction. Acronyms like that are common in NASA programs because they need to appeal to public and political interest. It's distracting and unnecessary to a fictional story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The lackluster feeling is kind of intentional, the Foundation knows something big is there but it can't properly see it because our galaxy's in the way. So the article is written as more of a 'calm before the storm' kind of deal, the Foundation knows what's coming and has to begin to prepare to face something they can only see by its effects on galaxy-sized structures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The general premise is that there is an unseen gravitational anomaly which is attracting galaxies towards it due to its high-mass concentration (similar in principle to a black hole but on a far larger scale) and subsequently tearing galaxy sized structures to pieces. Which is definitely something which should not be happening.

Daw, come on. The acronym is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
High-tech sci-fi skips can be a hard sell. What I'm getting from this is either a bunch of galaxies are coming to call, which is definitely not a thing that should be happening, and/or something is speeding through the supercluster, wrecking everything in its galaxy-sized wake. Yes?

DEep spaCE anoMalous oBservER (DECEMBER)

I mean this lovingly, but this hurt. D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. You make it ominous without giving it some sort of personified attributes or clear malice. It just is, and it screwed up an entire galaxy in less than 12 hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You did it you bastard. And to think we didn't have any authors in our server.

+1

EDIT: I meant for this to just be a response and not a reply, dammit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is competently well-written but it feels lackluster, especially for something to do with the Great Attractor. It feels to be slightly reiterating what is actually known but with slightly different details. The part that was interesting is it quickly moving a galaxy and scattering it about. Other than that, I don't feel like I gain anything from reading this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, here’s my first published SCP article. Thanks to those of you on the #site19 IRC channel who reviewed it.
